segments.s689-g49: This is a noun phrase subject: 'سکوبا و رهبان' (two coordinated nouns) followed by a prepositional directional phrase 'سوی شهریار'. In classical Persian the preposition 'سوی' (= 'toward') comes before its object; the whole phrase sets up who moves and in whose direction.
segments.s689-g50: 'برفتند' is past tense, 3rd person plural (they went / departed), with the older prefix 'بر-' preserved in poetry. The prepositional phrase 'با هدیه و با نثار' follows as instrument/accompaniment: 'with a gift and with an offering' (the repetition of با is emphatic/poetic). 'نثار' here is a noun meaning 'offering/tribute'.
segments.s689-g51: 'همی‌رفت' is an imperfective/habitual past narrative form (literally 'kept going' / 'was going' / 'would go'); here it functions to narrate ongoing action. 'با شاه' is the phrase indicating the target/addressee (in this construction 'با' can mean 'to/with' someone when speech is involved). 'چندی سخن' is a quantifier + noun: 'a few words' or 'some talk' (چندی modifies سخن).
segments.s689-g52: The preposition 'ز' (poetic form of 'از') introduces the topic of the speech: 'ز باران و آن شارستان کهن' = 'about/ concerning the rain and that ancient city.' Word order is topic ('باران') + conjunction + demonstrative + noun + adjective ('آن شارستان کهن').
segments.s689-g53: 'همی‌گفت' is a habitual/narrative past ('kept saying' / 'would say'). 'هرکس' here functions as the subject 'each/ everyone' (each person used to say). The clause introduced by 'که' is the content of their speech: 'ما بنده‌ایم' = 'we are (humble) servants' — 'بنده‌ایم' is 1st-person plural present used in reported speech.
segments.s689-g54: 'به گفتار خسرو' is an agent/occasion phrase: 'at/by the word/saying of the king' — here 'به' expresses relation/agency ('by/at'), 'گفتار' = 'speech/word', and 'خسرو' = the sovereign. 'سر افگنده‌ایم' is a fixed verbal phrase: 'سر افگندن' = 'to lower/bow the head'; 'افگنده‌ایم' is perfective (past participle + auxiliary) meaning 'we have cast/down/bowed (our) heads.'
segments.s689-g9: فروماند is a past/intransitive verb meaning 'was left / remained behind' (here describing the king); زان = 'from that / from him' introduces reference to the preceding person; شاه گیتی فروز is a noun phrase with an epithet after the head: شاه = 'king', گیتی فروز = 'world-illuminating / glorious of the world' (adjective phrase following شاه). The verb comes first for emphasis, then the referent and its epithet follow.
segments.s689-g10: به بیرون is an adverbial phrase meaning 'outdoors / outside'; بماندند is 3rd-person plural past 'they remained' (the imperfective/past narrative form), so the clause reads 'they remained outside'; لشکر is the subject 'the army/host' and روز 'day' with the numeral/quantifier 'سه' after it gives the duration 'for three days' (classical word order places the time after the verb phrase).
segments.s689-g11: فرستاد is 3rd-person singular past ('he sent' / 'he dispatched'); روز چهارم is a temporal adverbial phrase 'on the fourth day' that follows the verb here; کسی is the direct object 'someone' (the person sent). This order (verb + time + object) is common in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s689-g12: که is a relative/conjunction 'who/that' linking back to کسی; نزدیک ما نیست is a verbal clause 'is not near us' (نزدیک = 'near', ما = 'us', نیست = 'is not'); لشکر بسی follows as an appositive/afterthought meaning 'a great/considerable army' with بسی used in a poetic sense to indicate largeness—word order is inverted for poetic effect. Together it conveys '(someone) who is not close to us — (there is) a large army.'
segments.s689-g13: خورشها is the plural definite/indefinite 'dishes / victuals' (‑ها plural suffix); فرستید and کنید are 2nd-person plural imperatives/optatives meaning 'send' and 'do / give' respectively; the two verbs are coordinated by و 'and', so the command is 'send victuals and help (us)'.
segments.s689-g14: چه here functions as a causal/concessive conjunction 'for / because' introducing the reason or desired outcome; برما = 'to us / upon us' marks the beneficiary; همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle ('indeed / in this very'); کامگاری is a noun 'success, bringing of fulfillment', and کنید is again 2nd-person plural 'do' — together 'for you may make us successful / do us this favor'.
segments.s689-g15: به نزدیک ایشان = 'near them' (prepositional phrase with به + نزدیک + pronoun ایشان). سخن خوار بود is a nominal+adjective copular clause: سخن 'speech/talk' + خوار 'despised/low' + بود 'was' yielding 'speech was contemptible/they were spoken of contemptuously' (خوار as predicate adjective after the noun).
segments.s689-g16: سپاهش is the compound of سپاه 'army' + suffix ش 'his' = 'his army'; همه 'all' modifies the following adjective سست 'weak' (word order 'all weak'); و 'and' links the second clause ناهار بود = 'there was a noonday meal / they had lunch' with بود as the past copula. The line states that his entire force was lax/weak and they were at midday meal.
segments.s689-g41: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آباد جایی is a noun phrase 'a prosperous/inhabited place' with آباد adjectival before the noun جایی (old/poetic adjective–noun order). به چنگ آمدش is a light‑verb construction: به + چنگ (into/into the grip) + آمدش (آمد + enclitic -ش 'to him/it'), literally 'came into his hand/grasp.'
segments.s689-g42: برآسود is a past/perfective verbal adjective/verb meaning 'was at rest / took ease.' و is the coordinating 'and.' چندی درنگ آمدش is an idiomatic sequence: چندی 'for a while' + درنگ 'a pause/delay' + آمدش (came to him) giving 'he tarried / lingered for a while.' The verb آمدش again carries the enclitic -ش 'to him.'
segments.s689-g43: به قیصر is the prepositional phrase 'to Caesar' (به + object). یکی نامه is 'a certain letter' or 'one letter' (indefinite + noun). بنوشت here is the past tense verb 'wrote' (an older narrative past form of نوشت), and شاه is the subject 'the king' placed after the verb in the poet's word order.
segments.s689-g44: ازان is a deictic/relational particle 'of/from that' linking the following list; باد و باران و ابر سیاه is a coordinated noun phrase 'wind and rain and a black cloud.' Here the conjunctions join weather‑words describing the matter of the letter or the calamity.
segments.s689-g45: وزان 'and/from that' establishes continuity with the previous clause. شارستان 'the town/city' is the direct object/topic. سوی مانوی راند is a verb phrase: سوی + مانوی 'toward the Manichean' and راند 'he drove/forced (it/them)', with the verb rاند in simple past narrative position at the end.
segments.s689-g46: که introduces an explanatory clause 'for/that' (here: 'for the sovereign called it ...'). آن را is the resumed direct object 'it' marked by را. جهاندار مانوی خواند: جهاندار 'sovereign' modifies مانوی (a Manichean name/epithet) and خواند is used in the sense 'called/named' (to call someone/something by a name).
segments.s689-g47: زما نوییان is a possessive/partitive construction 'our new ones / our newcomers' (زما = our). هرک بیدار بود literally 'whoever was awake/alert' — هرک is an archaic distributive 'whoever' and بود is the past copula 'was.'
segments.s689-g48: This is a predicate nominal series linked to بود: خردمند و راد و جهاندار بود = 'was wise and generous (rād) and a sovereign.' Adjectives/nouns are coordinated with و and the final بود ties them to the subject introduced in the previous segment.
segments.s689-g25: ساختند is the past-plural verb 'they made/they built'. The phrase بهر بر زنی is a beneficiary expression ('for the woman') in a poetic word order (بهر + preposition + noun) and بر علف is a locative phrase 'on/with grass' placed after the beneficiary; together they mean 'they made (something) for the woman on/with grass.'
segments.s689-g26: سه modifies the following nominal phrase 'پیر سکوبا' ('three old sokubā'); سکوبا is an obscure term (read here as a sort of attendant/cupbearer). برون is the adverb 'out/forth' and تاختند is the past plural verb 'they rushed/charged out.'
segments.s689-g27: ز at the start functions here as a preposition 'from' or introducing the thing in question; چیزی که بود is a relative clause 'something that was', with بود as simple past 'was'. اندران ('in it' / 'therein') locates that something inside the place, and تازه بوم is a compound noun phrase 'new/fresh dwelling'. The sequence follows older poetic ordering where location and modifier appear after the clause.
segments.s689-g28: همان is the demonstrative 'the same/the very', followed by جامه‌هایی (plural 'garments'). که introduces a relative clause; خیزد is classical/poetic present (or aresultative sense) 'arise/come' applied here to origin — 'that come/arose from', and ز روم marks origin 'from Rome' (i.e. Byzantium).
segments.s689-g29: ببردند is a past plural verb 'they took/carried' (here with implication 'carried up'), modified by بالا ('upwards') and the prepositional phrase به نزدیک شاه 'to (the) near[ness] of the king' meaning 'to the king's presence'. Word order places adverbials before the verbal action.
segments.s689-g30: که here introduces a consequent clause 'when/that it became apparent'; پیدا شده (پیدا + شد) = 'became apparent/was revealed'. The following direct address ای شاه 'O king' introduces the quoted statement برما گناه 'the sin/blame is upon us' (برما = 'on/upon us', گناه = 'fault/blame').
segments.s689-g31: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; خسرو جوان بود is a simple past clause 'Khosrow was young'. The phrase و برتر منش ('and of superior/noble character') is an adjectival phrase appended to the clause (the copula 'was' is implied for the second predicate), typical of classical Persian elision.
segments.s689-g32: بدیشان is a dative/enclitic-like phrase 'to them' (i.e. concerning them); نکرد is the past negative 'did not do'. از here marks the reason/ground 'because of/for' and links to بدی ('evil/wrongdoing') as the cause, with سرزنش 'reproach' as the object: 'he did not reproach them for their wrongdoing.'
segments.s689-g1: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when'). بگذشت is past tense (3rd singular) used here with لشکر as the logical subject following the verb — a common poetic inversion: 'when the army passed'. بران is an adverbial/deictic 'on/thereupon' pointing to the place just mentioned; تازه بوم is a noun phrase 'fresh/new land' (بوم = homeland/soil).
segments.s689-g2: بتندی is a past/stative verb meaning 'hastened' or 'pressed on' (here accelerated movement). همی‌راند is iterative/progressive past ('kept driving' / 'drove on'), with the verb in permissive/continuous poetic form. تا introduces a spatial limit ('as far as' / 'up to'), followed by مرز روم 'the frontier of Rome' as the goal/destination.
segments.s689-g3: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' setting the manner. تا + past verb بیامد functions as a temporal/completive clause 'until they came' or idiomatically 'as far as they reached'. بران again points to 'that' place, and شارستان is the city reached; the verb (بیامد) governs the clause though the agent is implicit/plural (they).
segments.s689-g4: که is a relative/conjunction 'which/that' linking to the preceding clause. قیصر is subject 'Caesar'. ورا here is the object pronoun 'it/that' (referring to the city or what they did), and خواندی is past tense 'called/named' (here 'called it...'). کارستان is the predicative noun 'a feat / a marvel' — together: 'which Caesar called a marvel.'
segments.s689-g5: چواز is a compound/poetic conjunction meaning 'when/after/from' (temporal), followed by دور 'from afar'. ترسا is a noun 'ascetic/pious man' (object seen). بدید is the past of 'to see'; the subject آن سپاه ('that army') follows the verb — poetic inversion common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s689-g6: برفتند is simple past plural 'they departed / went off'. پویان is an adverbial/participle meaning 'moving/energetic' (here 'moving on' / 'in motion'). به marks direction 'toward/into' and آبی راه ('watery road' = waterway) is the destination; the doubled راه…راه is poetic repetition for emphasis/continuation.
segments.s689-g7: بدان باره is a fixed temporal phrase 'on that occasion' (ب + دان + باره combining into the adverbial). اندر is a locative/prepositional particle 'into/within'. کشیدند is past plural 'they drew/pulled' and رخت here means 'baggage/equipment' (so 'they drew in their baggage').
segments.s689-g8: در in this line is the noun 'gate' (not the preposition) — 'the gate of the city'. شارستان names the city; را is the definite/object marker attached to the whole object phrase; ببستند is past plural 'they closed/shut', and سخت functions adverbially 'firmly/forcefully'.
segments.s689-g17: هم آنگه is an adverbial phrase setting the time: هم (an emphatic/also-type particle) + آنگه ('then, at that moment'). برآمد is a past tense, 3rd-person singular verb 'arose/appeared'. یکی تیره ابر is an indefinite noun phrase (یکی = 'a certain/one', تیره = adjective 'dark', ابر = 'cloud') placed after the verb in poetic word order but together forming the subject that has risen.
segments.s689-g18: بغرید is a past, intransitive verb 'it roared/bellowed'. برسان here functions as a gerund-like or resultative connector 'bringing/bringing about' (classical/poetic usage) that links the roar to what follows. جنگی هژبر is a noun with an epithet: جنگی ('warlike, warrior') modifies the proper name هژبر (Hžabr/Hojabr).
segments.s689-g19: وز (here a noun 'a gust/blast' used like 'from') + ابر اندران = 'from that cloud / from within the cloud' (اندران = 'therein, from within it'). شارستان ('town, city') names the place affected, and باد خاست is the main clause: باد = 'wind', خاست = past 'arose/began to blow'. Overall: 'From that cloud over the city a wind arose.'
segments.s689-g20: بهر بر زنی is a prepositional phrase meaning 'toward/for a woman' (بهر = 'for/to/toward', بر = 'upon/toward', زنی = 'a woman'). بانگ و فریاد خاست is a compound subject + verb: 'a cry and an outcry arose' (بانگ = 'cry', و = conjunction 'and', فریاد = 'cry/shout', خاست = 'arose').
segments.s689-g21: چونیمی is an adverbial/temporal particle meaning roughly 'as (if) half/when', introducing the time clause. ز تیره شب اندر کشید is the verb phrase: ز = 'from', تیره شب = 'the dark night', اندر کشید = past of کشیدن used here intransitively 'drew in / spread across' (i.e. darkness spread). The clause says that darkness drew in.
segments.s689-g22: ز باره is a circumstantial phrase 'on that occasion / with that burst/at that moment' (باره used poetically for an instance/occasion). یکی بهره شد is a compact phrase where یکی = 'one (of them)', بهره = 'portion/one of the company' (poetically 'one person/one part'), شد = past 'became', and ناپدید = adjective 'vanished/gone'. Together: 'one of them became vanished.'
segments.s689-g23: همه شارستان is the subject 'the whole town / all the city'. ماند is the past 'remained/was left'. اندر شگفت is a locative/state phrase 'in astonishment' (شگفت used as noun 'wonder/amazement'). Word order is subject — verb — locative complement.
segments.s689-g24: به یزدان is a prepositional goal 'to God / toward God'. سقف پوزش is a poetic collocation: سقف ('roof/top') used metaphorically with پوزش ('pardon/forgiveness') to mean a lifted or supreme plea for mercy. اندر گرفت is the verbal phrase 'took into / seized upon' (گرفت = past 'took/seized'); together the phrase means they directed a plea for pardon to God (i.e. 'they lifted a plea to God asking forgiveness').
segments.s689-g33: بدان شارستان = a locative phrase: بدَان (به + آن) = 'to/at that' placed before شارستان ('city') for poetic word order, so 'in/at that city'. دریکی کاخ بود is a simple past clause: در یکی 'in one (of the quarters / a single)' + کاخ 'palace' as subject and بود 'was' as the past copula—literally 'in that city there was a palace (in one [place])'.
segments.s689-g34: که introduces a relative clause modifying کاخ ('which/whose'). بالاش is بالا + -ش (possessive enclitic) = 'its upper part/summit'. با ابر گستاخ بود is a copular description: با 'with' + ابر گستاخ 'an audacious/impudent cloud' (noun + postpositive adjective) + بود 'was' (past), so 'its summit was with/an audacious cloud' → 'its summit was audaciously shrouded in clouds.'
segments.s689-g35: فراوان is an adverb/adjective 'abundantly/many' placed before the indirect object. بدو is به + او/آن ('to it/him') functioning as the indirect object/target. اندرون برده بود is a compound past: اندرون 'inside' (locative) + برده (past participle 'taken/brought') + بود (auxiliary past) = 'had been brought inside.' The structure yields 'many had been brought inside to it.'
segments.s689-g36: همان جای = 'that very place/spot' (همان = demonstrative modifier of جای). قیصر names the possessor/beneficiary: 'the Caesar's place' (poetic omission of ezafe is common). برآورده بود is برآورده (past participle with sense 'provided/appointed') + بود (past auxiliary) describing a resultant state: 'had been provided/appointed.'
segments.s689-g37: ز دشت is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the plain' (ز = 'from'). اندرآمد here functions as a past verb 'came in/entered' (verb phrase following the prepositional phrase). بدَنجا (بدانجا) = به + آنجا 'to that place' and گذشت is a past verb 'passed/went by', so the clause reads 'he came from the plain and went to that place.'
segments.s689-g38: فراوان here is an adverb modifying the following motion/return. بدان شارستان = به + آن + شارستان 'to that city'. دربَگَشت is a verbal/nominal form meaning 'return/turning back' (used here as a past action 'returned'); together: 'he returned to that city in great number / abundantly.'
segments.s689-g39: همه رومیان = 'all the Romans' (quantifier + noun). آفرین خواندند is a light-object + verb: آفرین (an exclamation 'bravo/praise') + خواندند (past plural of خواندن 'to utter/read/sing'); here خواندند means 'they exclaimed/uttered', so 'all the Romans cried "bravo".'
segments.s689-g40: بپا is an intransitive/exclamatory verb/adverb meaning 'stand up/rise' used here as a short sequence marker ('they rose'). اندرش = اندر + -ش ('in/at' + 'him/its') = 'around him/upon him'. گوهر افشاندند is 'they scattered/sprinkled jewels': گوهر 'gems' (direct object) + افشاندند past plural 'they scattered', so 'they rose and showered jewels upon him.'

sentences.s689-s25: The cup-bearer and the monk went toward the king, bearing gifts and offerings.
sentences.s689-s26: They went to the king and spoke briefly — words about the rain and that ancient city.
sentences.s689-s27: Each one kept saying, 'We are humble servants,' and (that) at the king's word we have bowed our heads.
sentences.s689-s5: That world-illuminating king was left behind; the army remained outside for three days.
sentences.s689-s6: On the fourth day he sent someone, (saying) that the army is not near us in force.
sentences.s689-s7: Send victuals and give aid, for do us this favor (bring us success).
sentences.s689-s8: Near them the talk was low/despised; his whole army was weak, and there was the noonday meal.
sentences.s689-s21: When a prosperous place came into his grasp, he relaxed and lingered there for a while.
sentences.s689-s22: The king wrote a letter to Caesar about that wind and rain and the black cloud.
sentences.s689-s23: From that he drove the town toward the Manichaean (group), for the sovereign called it 'Manavi.'
sentences.s689-s24: Among our newcomers, whoever was alert was wise, generous, and a sovereign.
sentences.s689-s13: They made a bed of grass for the woman, and three old sokubā rushed out.
sentences.s689-s14: There was something in that new dwelling — the very garments that had come from Rome.
sentences.s689-s15: They carried her up to the king's presence; when it became apparent they cried, 'O king, the blame is on us.'
sentences.s689-s16: When Khosrow was young and of superior disposition, he did not reproach them for their wrongdoing.
sentences.s689-s1: When the army passed that fresh land, they hastened on, driving (their mounts) as far as the Roman frontier.
sentences.s689-s2: Thus they proceeded until they came to that city, which Caesar called a marvel.
sentences.s689-s3: When that army saw the ascetic from a distance, they set off, moving toward the waterway.
sentences.s689-s4: On that occasion they drew in their baggage; they firmly shut the city gate.
sentences.s689-s9: Then a dark cloud arose; it roared, bringing the warlike Hžabr (Hojabr).
sentences.s689-s10: From that cloud a gust of wind rose over the town; cries and shouts for a woman arose.
sentences.s689-s11: As the dark night drew in, at that instant one of them vanished.
sentences.s689-s12: The whole town was left in astonishment; they turned to God, lifting a plea for pardon.
sentences.s689-s17: In that city there stood a single palace, whose summit was audaciously shrouded in clouds.
sentences.s689-s18: Many had been brought inside to it; that very place had been provided for the Caesar.
sentences.s689-s19: He came out of the plain and went to that place; then he returned to that city in great numbers.
sentences.s689-s20: All the Romans cried 'bravo'; they rose and showered jewels upon him.
